title: Charts
summary: The visuals that show up on a PMBOK 8 PMP exam, in scan-friendly blocks: what each chart is for, how to read it, and which performance domain and principle it serves. Charts make monitoring and controlling visible - reliable data that drives timely decisions and protects value.

sections.heading: Why charts matter on the exam
sections.body: PMBOK 8 is principle- and outcome-based. Charts are tools you tailor and use to hit performance-domain outcomes - they are not the answer by themselves. Most chart questions are really monitoring-and-controlling questions: turn data into an actionable, value-protecting decision.

blocks.title: How charts map to PMBOK 8
lines: Monitoring and controlling: reliable status and actionable data drive timely decisions.
Scope domain: quality charts confirm deliverables meet scope and quality.
Risk domain: trend charts surface threats early.
Embed Quality principle: build quality in; measure to prevent, not just detect.
Tailoring: pick the chart that fits the delivery approach - do not force one.

blocks.title: Chart -> what it answers -> where it fits

rows.value: Which few causes drive most defects? -> Scope / Quality.
Is the process stable and in control? -> Monitoring & controlling / Quality.
How much work is left this sprint? -> Schedule / monitoring.
How much is done, and did scope change? -> Scope / monitoring.
What are the possible root causes? -> Risk / Scope.

sections.heading: Pareto chart
sections.body: A bar chart of causes ordered from most to least frequent, with a line showing the running cumulative percentage. It is a quality tool used to find which few causes drive most of the problems, so you fix those first. It supports the Scope domain (deliverables meeting quality) and the Embed Quality principle by focusing prevention where it pays off.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='18' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='52' y='52' width='34' height='108' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='100' y='82' width='34' height='78' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='148' y='112' width='34' height='48' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='196' y='134' width='34' height='26' fill='#1F5FA8'/><rect x='244' y='148' width='34' height='12' fill='#1F5FA8'/><polyline points='69,50 117,32 165,23 213,19 261,17' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='69' cy='50' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='117' cy='32' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='165' cy='23' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='213' cy='19' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='261' cy='17' r='3' fill='#B5462F'/><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>causes — most frequent first</text></svg>
figure.caption: Bars: frequency (descending). Line: cumulative %.

groups.label: Bars
lines: Frequency of each cause, tallest on the left, descending to the right.

groups.label: Line
lines: Cumulative percentage climbing left to right, flattening near 100 percent.

groups.label: Decision
lines: Attack the tall left-hand bars first for the biggest quality gain.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Pareto = the 80/20 rule: focus on the 'vital few' causes on the left, not the 'trivial many' on the right.
It is a bar chart, so it ranks discrete categories - not a trend over time.
Often paired after a fishbone: fishbone finds candidate causes, Pareto ranks the ones that actually occur.

sections.heading: Control chart
sections.body: A run of measurements over time with a centre line (the process mean) and an upper and lower control limit (UCL / LCL). It tells you whether a process is stable and 'in control'. This is monitoring and controlling in action: distinguish normal variation from a signal worth investigating.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='30' x2='300' y2='30' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='40' y1='150' x2='300' y2='150' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 4'/><line x1='40' y1='90' x2='300' y2='90' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><polyline points='55,96 82,78 109,102 136,74 163,96 190,70 217,100 244,22 271,86' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='55' cy='96' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='82' cy='78' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='109' cy='102' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='136' cy='74' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='163' cy='96' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='190' cy='70' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='217' cy='100' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><circle cx='244' cy='22' r='4' fill='#B5462F'/><circle cx='271' cy='86' r='3' fill='#1F5FA8'/><text x='304' y='30' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>UCL</text><text x='304' y='150' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>LCL</text><text x='304' y='86' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>mean</text></svg>
figure.caption: The red point above the UCL is out of control.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: Points inside the control limits.
Randomly scattered around the mean.

groups.label: Action
lines: Leave it alone - only common-cause (normal) variation is present.

groups.label: Pattern
lines: A point beyond UCL or LCL.
A non-random run (rule of seven).

groups.label: Action
lines: Investigate the special cause; assign and remove it.

items.text: Upper / lower control limits - the boundary of expected variation (set from the process, usually +/- 3 sigma).
Customer requirement limits - not the same as control limits; control limits are narrower and process-driven.
Seven points in a row on one side of the mean signals a non-random trend, even inside the limits.
Normal, inherent variation; do not react to it.
An assignable, unusual event to investigate.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Out of control = a point beyond UCL/LCL, OR the rule of seven.
Do not confuse control limits (process voice) with specification limits (customer voice).
Reacting to common-cause variation ('tampering') makes a stable process worse.

sections.heading: Burndown chart
sections.body: An agile chart showing work remaining over a sprint or release. The straight 'ideal' line runs from all the work down to zero; the 'actual' line is the real remaining work each day. It is a Schedule-domain progress artifact tailored to an adaptive approach, giving the team fast, self-managed feedback.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='44' y1='34' x2='296' y2='158' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><polyline points='44,34 90,52 136,86 182,96 228,128 296,158' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='52' fill='currentColor' font-size='10'>ideal</text><text x='150' y='80' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10'>actual</text><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>sprint days</text></svg>
figure.caption: Work remaining vs the ideal line, across the sprint.

groups.label: Ideal line
lines: Straight line from total work down to zero at sprint end.

groups.label: Actual above ideal
lines: More work remaining than planned = behind.

groups.label: Actual below ideal
lines: Less work remaining than planned = ahead.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Burndown shows work REMAINING, trending DOWN toward zero.
A burn-up chart instead shows work COMPLETED against total scope, trending UP.
Burndown hides scope changes - a flat line could mean added scope offsetting progress; use burn-up to tell them apart.

sections.heading: Burn-up chart
sections.body: The companion to the burndown. Instead of work remaining, it plots work completed (rising) against a separate line for total scope. Because scope is its own line, you can see scope changes that a burndown would hide - directly supporting the Scope domain's focus on deliverables and realised value.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 190' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.5'/><polyline points='44,60 170,60 170,42 296,42' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.6' stroke-dasharray='5 4'/><polyline points='44,158 92,140 140,110 188,96 236,70 296,46' stroke='#2F6F5E' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='36' fill='#B5462F' font-size='10'>scope</text><text x='150' y='118' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10'>completed</text><text x='170' y='182' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>sprint days</text></svg>
figure.caption: Completed work rising toward total scope (which steps up when scope is added).

groups.label: Completed line
lines: Rises over time toward the scope line.

groups.label: Scope line
lines: Steps up when work is added, down when removed.

groups.label: Done when
lines: The completed line meets the scope line.

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Burn-up separates progress from scope - a jump in the top line means scope was ADDED, not that the team slowed down.
Prefer burn-up when stakeholders question scope creep or need transparency on added work.
The gap between the two lines is the remaining work.

sections.heading: Cause-and-effect (fishbone / Ishikawa)
sections.body: A root-cause tool. The 'effect' (the problem) sits at the head; angled 'bones' group possible causes into categories, with finer sub-causes branching off each. It structures a team's brainstorming so you investigate causes by category rather than at random - a Risk- and Scope-domain aid to understanding a problem before responding.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='16' y1='100' x2='266' y2='100' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.6'/><polygon points='266,94 278,100 266,106' fill='currentColor'/><rect x='280' y='84' width='52' height='32' rx='4' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='306' y='104' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Effect</text><line x1='70' y1='44' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='44' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='70' y1='156' x2='104' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='170' y1='156' x2='204' y2='100' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='66' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>People</text><text x='166' y='38' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Process</text><text x='66' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Tools</text><text x='166' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='middle'>Environment</text></svg>
figure.caption: Causes grouped on bones; the problem (effect) at the head.

rows.value: Machine, Method, Material, Manpower, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
People, Process, Tools, Environment (as in the classic diagram).

blocks.title: Exam tip
lines: Fishbone = Ishikawa = cause-and-effect diagram (three names, one tool).
It EXPLORES possible causes; it does not PROVE them - pair it with data (e.g. Pareto or 5 Whys) to confirm.
Effect at the head (the problem); causes on the bones.

sections.heading: Chart cheat lines
sections.body: One line each for the fastest possible recall in the exam.

rows.value: Pareto - focus on the vital few.
Control chart - stable vs special cause.

rows.key: Line rising to a scope line

blocks.title: Final tip
lines: Pick the chart the delivery approach and the question actually call for - tailoring, not habit.
A chart's job is a timely decision that protects value, not a pretty picture.
When two answers fit, choose the one that turns the data into action on the vital few.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and reorganised into structured blocks for the current PMBOK 8, principle- and outcome-based PMP exam.
